intent: ADVERSARIAL verdict-discrimination: an audited `avc: granted { read }` record (an allow that SUCCEEDED) mixed with an `avc: denied { write }` record in the same set. triage must distinguish the verdict (avc.c:659 emits granted vs denied) and emit an allow ONLY for the actual denial, never for the granted record.
_kinds_note: authoritative_category in {TeAllowable, Constraint, Bounds} (libsepol/audit2why replay); floor_label in {none, Permissive, MlsSuspected, RoleSuspected} (record-only heuristic). Applies to the DENIED write only; the granted read is excluded by verdict before any categorization.

oracle.audit2allow_N: allow logrotate_t var_log_t:file write;  (audit2allow correctly SKIPS the granted read - prints '#!!!! This avc is allowed in the current policy' for it - and emits an allow only for the denied write)
oracle.audit2allow_R: logging_rw_generic_logs(logrotate_t)  (the -R macro form for the denied write only; the granted read is still skipped)
oracle.narrower_than_R_by: For the denied write: -R substitutes the logging_rw_generic_logs() interface macro (grants read+write generic-log perms, likely on multiple log types), whereas expected.te emits exactly `allow logrotate_t var_log_t:file write;` - the single denied perm on the single denied type, no macro, no read padding, no extra log types.
oracle.audit2why_category: TeAllowable (the denied write is a missing-TE-allow gap; the granted read audit2why labels as 'should be dontaudit'd' i.e. already allowed)

triage_expectation.suspected_trap: A naive impl that ignores the verdict token and treats every `type=AVC` record as a denial would emit a bogus `allow logrotate_t shadow_t:file read;` for the GRANTED read (which already succeeded and needs no fix), in addition to the real write allow. Correct behavior: filter on `granted` vs `denied` (avc.c:659) and only suggest fixes for denials. audit2allow gets this right (it skips the grant); a hand-rolled parser must too.
